learn.title: Rooftop Solar & Net Metering
learn.intro: Rooftop solar harnesses sunlight on homes/businesses, reducing grid reliance. Net metering lets excess power feed the grid, lowering bills. This empowers Indians to combat fossil fuel dependence, cut emissions, and foster energy independence, crucial for India's 2030 renewable targets.
learn.c1_text: Rooftop panels convert sunlight to electricity via photovoltaic cells. Imagine a 1 kW panel generating ~4 units daily in India. This offsets grid use, saving ₹3,000-₹5,000 monthly. Inverters then convert DC to AC for home use, making solar viable even in urban areas with limited space.
learn.c2_text: Net metering allows excess solar power to be sent to the grid, crediting users. In India, states like Gujarat offer 1:1 credit ratios. For a 5 kW system generating 20 units/month, users save ₹1,500+ in bills. This policy incentivizes adoption by turning homes into mini power stations.
learn.c3_text: India has 10 GW rooftop solar installed (2024), targeting 40 GW by 2030. Net metering in Rajasthan reduced diesel use by 1.2 million liters annually. Policies like MNRE's subsidies make rooftop solar 20-30% cheaper, driving adoption in cities with high electricity tariffs.
learn.stat2: Rajasthan saved 1.2M liters diesel/year via net metering
learn.stat3: MNRE subsidies reduce rooftop costs by 20-30%

learn.title: Green Ammonia for Clean Shipping
learn.intro: Green ammonia, made from hydrogen and nitrogen using renewable energy, replaces fossil fuels in shipping and fertilizers. It cuts CO2 emissions by 70% compared to traditional methods, offering India a path to decarbonize its shipping sector and boost sustainable agriculture.
learn.c1_text: Green ammonia combines green hydrogen (from water electrolysis) with nitrogen. A 1 tonne batch requires 800 kWh of renewable energy. Unlike gray ammonia (from coal), it emits zero CO2 during production. This makes it ideal for shipping, where carbon-neutral fuels are critical.
learn.c2_text: Ships can use ammonia as fuel, reducing emissions by 70%. India's fertilizer sector could use green ammonia to cut 2.5M tonnes of CO2 annually. For example, a single ship using 100 tonnes/month could save ₹1.5 crore in carbon credits under India's carbon trading scheme.
learn.c3_text: India's fertilizer sector consumes 25M tonnes ammonia yearly. Switching to green ammonia could reduce 5M tonnes CO2/year. The government's Hydrogen Mission aims to produce 500,000 tonnes green ammonia by 2030, targeting exports to Europe and Southeast Asia.
learn.stat1: Green ammonia cuts shipping CO2 by 70%
learn.stat2: India's fertilizer sector uses 25M tonnes ammonia/year
learn.stat3: Hydrogen Mission targets 500,000 tonnes green ammonia by 2030

learn.title: Green Hydrogen via Electrolysis
learn.intro: Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water using renewable energy. PEM (Proton Exchange Membrane) and alkaline electrolysis differ in efficiency and cost. This technology is vital for India's hydrogen economy, enabling clean industrial processes and energy storage solutions.
learn.c1_text: PEM systems use a solid polymer membrane to split water. They operate at high pressures (350 bar) and efficiency (60-70%). A 1 MW PEM plant costs ₹1.2 crore but produces hydrogen faster, ideal for fluctuating solar/wind. For India, this suits urban areas with intermittent renewables.
learn.c2_text: Alkaline systems use liquid electrolytes and operate at lower pressures (10-20 bar). They’re cheaper (₹0.8 crore/MW) but less efficient (50-60%). Suitable for large-scale, stable renewable sources like solar farms in Rajasthan. Both methods are key to India’s 5M tonnes hydrogen target by 2030.
learn.c3_text: India aims for 5M tonnes green hydrogen by 2030. PEM is favored for industrial use in Gujarat, while alkaline suits rural solar parks. The National Hydrogen Mission allocates ₹19,744 crore, with 25% for electrolysis R&D. This could reduce India's $15B annual fossil fuel imports.
learn.stat1: India targets 5M tonnes green hydrogen by 2030

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels
learn.intro: Bifacial panels generate power from both sides, boosting efficiency by 10-25%. They’re ideal for India’s diverse landscapes, from deserts to farms. This technology maximizes land use, reduces costs, and aligns with India’s solar expansion goals.
learn.c1_text: Bifacial panels capture sunlight on both front and rear. A 1 kW panel in a desert can generate 1.2 kWh/day instead of 1 kWh. The rear side uses reflected light from sand or white surfaces. This increases energy yield by 15-20%, making them cost-effective for large projects.
learn.c2_text: In farmland, bifacial panels mounted on elevated structures harvest reflected light from crops. A 5 MW project in Gujarat saw 18% higher output. For industries, they reduce land use by 25%, ideal for space-constrained urban rooftops. This aligns with India's target to triple solar capacity by 2030.
learn.c3_text: India's bifacial adoption grew 40% in 2023. A 1 MW project in Rajasthan saved ₹2.5 crore annually via higher output. The MNRE offers 10% subsidies for bifacial installations. By 2025, 30% of new solar projects could use bifacial panels, cutting levelized costs by 12%.

learn.title: Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) Tech
learn.intro: V2G allows EVs to send stored energy back to the grid during peak demand. This stabilizes grids, reduces blackouts, and turns EVs into mobile batteries. For India, with a growing EV fleet and unstable grids, V2G offers a dual benefit of cleaner transport and reliable power.
learn.c1_text: V2G uses bidirectional chargers to transfer power. A 60 kWh EV battery can supply 5 kW for 10 hours. During peak hours, it feeds power to homes or industries. This avoids grid strain, similar to how a UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) works but at scale. For India, this is critical during monsoon outages.
learn.c2_text: V2G balances supply-demand fluctuations. A 100 MW V2G fleet can offset 200 MW of peak demand. In Germany, V2G reduced grid volatility by 30%. For India, integrating 1M EVs with V2G could stabilize 5 GW of grid capacity, cutting reliance on diesel generators during shortages.
learn.c3_text: India's EV fleet could reach 10M by 2030. V2G pilots in Tamil Nadu showed 15% grid stability improvement. The government's Faster Adoption and Manufacturing (FAM) scheme includes V2G incentives. By 2030, V2G could save ₹500 crore annually in grid maintenance costs.
learn.stat2: 100 MW V2G offsets 200 MW peak demand
learn.stat3: Tamil Nadu pilot improved stability by 15%

learn.title: How Electric Vehicles Work
learn.intro: EVs use electric motors instead of engines, powered by batteries. Regenerative braking recovers energy during braking, extending range. Charging options vary from home outlets to fast chargers. This technology reduces India's oil imports and air pollution, aligning with its clean mobility goals.
learn.c1_text: EVs use lithium-ion batteries (60-100 kWh) to power electric motors. A 40 kWh battery can drive 200 km. The motor converts electricity to motion, unlike internal combustion engines. For example, a Tata Nexon EV uses a 34 kWh battery for 312 km range. This eliminates tailpipe emissions.
learn.c2_text: When braking, the motor acts as a generator, converting kinetic energy to electricity. This can recover 15-20% of energy lost during braking. In city driving, this extends range by 10-15 km per charge. A Hyundai Kona Electric recovers 10 kWh/100 km, reducing charging frequency by 2-3 times weekly.
learn.c3_text: India has 10,000+ charging stations (2024), targeting 2.5L by 2026. Home charging costs ₹5-₹8/kWh, while fast chargers cost ₹10-₹15/kWh. The FAME II scheme subsidizes 40% of EV purchases. With 40M EVs projected by 2030, this could cut oil imports by 150M barrels/year.
